results.haiku_translation:
At age 18, after completing four years of study at the gymnasium in Stry, Lusia took her matriculation exams and received a government certificate.

Lusia first met Mumek (her future husband) when she was 16, when he came to visit his family in Bolkow. A girlfriend of hers, who was also Mumek's friend, introduced them. The three of them would go out together. One day Mumek invited her to go skiing with him. Her parents objected and the trip was cancelled.

A first meeting of just the two of them never took place.

The Family

Lusia was born into a wealthy family. Her father owned a leather factory, while her mother was a homemaker. She was the second daughter in the family and had two sisters and one brother.

Her eldest sister, Zippora Alder, may she rest in peace (mother of Akiva Alder, the political correspondent of "Ha'aretz"), married at a young age and immigrated to Palestine in 1932. She saved the entire family from the Holocaust (parents, sister and brother) by convincing them to immigrate to Palestine.

The parents' home was traditional and Zionist. The "Zionist atmosphere" was expressed in the education given to the children, according to which Eretz-Israel is the homeland of the Jewish people and it is a commandment to immigrate there to settle it. Fact: all family members, except Lusia, indeed immigrated to the land and were saved from the Holocaust.

There were also external signs of this in the home: the blue box of the Jewish National Fund and a box of the "Tel Hai Fund".

The family was observant. The Sabbath was welcomed with "Kiddush," challah and wine. Mother lit candles, and the candlelight shone from large, gleaming candlesticks. Everything was clean for "Queen Sabbath," who came to them in all her splendor.

She remembers Passover more than any other holiday. During this holiday they were especially careful to observe Jewish tradition. "Seder night" was festive and full

splendor. Mother would take out the dishes designated only for Passover. Each family member had a special cup, and Lusia's cup was shaped like a small jug, in dark blue color.

Father conducted the "Seder" according to law, following all the rules, both great and small. The children did not understand a word from the Haggadah reading, but they knew how to sing all the traditional Passover songs.

results.haiku_translation:
She was 18, he was 24. They married in a "white wedding" with a rabbi, without family members. Their wedding was held in the small resort town of Moshina, where they established their residence.

It was a very picturesque image of the young couple traveling to their wedding. They sat in a carriage drawn by horses, and the horses were galloping through the velvet of the snow.

Intense frost reigned outside, and the faces of the two were turned toward a Jewish rabbi in the diaspora, in a foreign land, who would join them together. They were happy with each other and had hopes for a good future, even though they were in the diaspora, among strangers.

Lusia was very innocent when she married. She did not know at all and did not understand at all what the concept of "a married woman" meant. Childbirth, motherhood and such—all these were ahead of her.

From a warm parental home, from a wonderful and innocent childhood, from the joy of youth and all goodness—she "fell" straight into marriage, pregnancy and childbirth.

After a year her firstborn son, Shimon, was born, and Lusia became a young and confused mother.

—

In Poland of those days—and actually in the entire Western world—it was customary to bake and cook by hand. Lusia learned to bake bread, cakes and pasta, and shortly thereafter her hand was skilled in all.

Lusia Goes to Work

Some time after the birth, Lusia came to the conclusion that it was not enough for her to be merely a mother and housewife, and she desired to earn money through self-employment. She said and she did.

She rented a magnificent hotel, worked in it and managed it. Most of the hotel's guests were Polish. Few were the Jews who stayed in it since the hotel did not maintain kosher.

• Note the detail about kashrut - the hotel did not keep kosher, which limited Jewish clientele. This reflects the secular environment of Soviet territory.

Lusia very much wanted to travel to Russia because of her fear of the Germans standing at the doorstep. She did not do so because of the opposition of her husband's parents and grandmother. results.haiku_translation:
Lusia, her husband, and their son Simon moved to live in the city of Nadworna, where the husband found work in a sawmill. They lived in a small neighborhood, next to the sawmill, together with the families of workers who worked at that factory. They lived in small houses. One room and a kitchen for each family. The toilets were outside the house. Near the house, each family had a large garden where they grew vegetables and various food crops. Some of the families also had chickens and cows. Each family cultivated their plot of land. The neighborly relations were nice and good. Thanks to the fact that Lusia's neighbors, who were more experienced than her in agricultural work, guided her and gave her manure to fertilize her garden.
Every morning she would go out to engage in agriculture: she would hoe her land and weed her garden of weeds. She loved working in the bosom of nature.
From time to time she would go visit her husband at the sawmill. She would stand beside him and gaze at the expanses of uncultivated land. The earth was covered with wild grass, green wild grass like a clover field and many acres of expanses. Continuing beyond the expanses of wild fields rose sturdy trees, clothed in green. It was a wild, wide forest that covered the horizon.
The Russians are leaving and the Germans are coming
In 1941 the war knocked at the door. Russian rule came into question. Strong winds of war were already blowing. Bad news came day after day, but everything was still distant.
One day suddenly shots were heard and immediately after that flames of fire were seen. Soon it became clear that the Russians, who feared the Germans would take over the city, had set fire to the railway cars at the station that were full of grain, flour and rice. They felt that control of Poland was slipping from their hands in the face of strengthening German threats.

The Poles understood that the war was at their doorstep and therefore hurried to save whatever could be saved. The rumors convinced the Poles that they must prepare for harder days ahead, and they prepared stocks of sacks of flour and sugar in their homes. The shop contained art objects and handcrafted works by rural artisans: artistic boxes, seashells, decorated plates, and various wooden carvings.

Shimon lived with his mother and all day he remained alone in the box, whose dimensions were 70 cm × 1.50 m. The box was his dwelling and his place of safety.
In the morning, before going to work, Lusia would leave food for him in the box, and in the evening, when she returned home, she would care for him, clean him, and give him all her love.

Shimon understood the horror of war. He felt every moment the sense of danger of death, hovering over him and his mother. Fears engulfed his childhood. Lusia was forced to explain to him that if he left the box, both of them would be lost, and the little one understood.
He was a sensitive and kind-hearted child. The sight of a flower that moved his heart delighted him. He saw countless horrors, but did not cry or scream. The world of war penetrated deep into his heart and his soul knew suffering and torment.

results.haiku_translation:
She knew she had to display confidence, so she walked with a forced smile on her face.
She stopped before a Gestapo official. She feared she would become confused, stammer, and her voice would tremble.
The Gestapo man looked at her with sharp eyes, from which wickedness shone forth.
He began to shower her with questions, one after another: "What is your name?" "How old are you?" "Where do you live?" "Where do you work?"
Lusia answered him with a clear Polish accent. She stood before him with an erect posture. Her appearance displayed firmness and confidence, but that is not what she felt in her heart.
Lusia controlled herself absolutely, despite the fear and terror before the Gestapo man, who was devoid of human feelings.
"You will receive the permission. You are able to travel on a railway car designated only for Germans," he said to her curtly as giving an order.
Lusia traveled with Mr. Donner in a car into which entry was permitted only for those of the Aryan race, where certainly they were not looking for Jews. Apparently, she was the only Jewess in this car.
She also successfully completed the task of purchasing art objects for the shop and returned happily home.

What Can a Watch Do?

One morning, on her way to work, she met the 14-year-old daughter of a lodger, Dr. Korner, a dentist from Landsberg, who was murdered by the Germans after they found gold in his house.
The girl walked in wooden clogs, despite the harsh winter, dressed in tattered clothes and looked absolutely miserable.
Lusia's heart ached for her and she showed her the shop where she worked and expressed willingness to help her as best she could. On a tiny note, she also wrote her address.

results.haiku_translation:
At night she did not close her eyes. She was holding her son, looking at his pale face and his withered body. Longing for them to be able to leave this hell and survive. She was awake and tense, waiting for something bad that could happen any moment.
The next morning she returned to work as if nothing had happened.

The Detectives on Her Trail...

Lusia went the next day to work on the usual route between her house and the shop.
Her eyes were burning and her whole body was worn and tired, oppressed and anxious. She was weak and longed for only a little rest.
As she walked, she heard men's footsteps behind her and she thought to herself: This is it! Here comes my end... Now they will take me... My path ends here... But no, I don't want to die...
She gathered her strength and her mighty will to live, and for that, to continue and fight. She increased her pace, reached the shop door, stretched her hand toward the entrance door and opened the shop as every morning.
"I must speak to you about something," said a thick, authoritative and aggressive voice of one of the two men who entered the shop after her. He continued immediately: "We suspect that you are not a Pole."
Lusia took a deep breath. Quietly she handed them the documents that were in her satchel and said: "Look and examine for yourselves. Here, please..."
The two took the documents, examined them, turned them over from side to side, but everything was correct. This was a Polish document without any flaw...
They did not know how to respond. They looked at each other and again examined the documents.
All that time, Lusia stood watching them, ready for the possibility that she would be asked questions. She knew that she would stand up to the questions with courage and without fear as long as she remained alive for the sake of her son and for herself.

Maria Ciccelik—the Polish woman from whom Lusia received her birth certificate, which served as the basis for her other documents and was a resident of Nadworna—was a nurse in Lvov.

This Maria decided that in honor of Christmas she would travel from Nadworna to spend the holiday with her sister. She came with her young daughter, who was about Shimon's age. While in Lvov, she came to visit Lusia, and Lusia was happy to host her and was grateful for all she had done for her.

While they were sitting and talking, there came strong, rhythmic knocking on the door. Lusia went pale and her heart began to beat rapidly.

When the door opened, two men in civilian clothes entered with large strides, as if marching in a military parade, and said: "We are from the Criminal Police, show us your documents."

Lusia gave them an identity document and a work document. The real Maria Ciccelik from Nadworna was a peasant woman, dressed simply, with black hair and eyes black as coal. Her appearance was that of a Jewess, and she had no documents.

The Germans looked at Maria and asked her: "What is your name?" She was afraid to answer and so remained silent.

The Germans did not let up, and in response to a repeated question, she claimed her name was Maria Ciccelik.

One looked at the other and said to them in crude language: "It doesn't matter which one of you is Jewish—both of you will be destroyed..."

She rarely saw her husband. He lived in a small hiding place next to the factory. Danger hovered over him hour by hour, day by day. Many Jews had hidden themselves, for it was dangerous to walk about in the streets.

One day, on her way to work, as she was descending the stairs of her building, she saw how the Germans seized a young man, dragged him into the stairwell of her building, stripped him of his underwear, and immediately began to beat him with murderous blows.

Yes, it was certainly a Jew! Lusia, the "Polishwoman," did not stop for a moment and continued on her way. In fact, the Poles too were very afraid of the Germans. They feared them and hated them at the same time. Poles who had hidden Jews knew that in doing so they were risking their lives.

On the other hand, there were Ukrainians and Poles who were collaborators, who, in order to gain their beloved drink—vodka—were willing to inform even on Poles who had hidden Jews. This was denunciation in exchange for a bottle.

On her way to work or to the market to buy something, Lusia saw every day these scenes of horror.

One day, when she went to the market, near the "Balaclava," the following image revealed itself to her eyes: a young Jewish man, wounded and bleeding, lying on the sidewalk floor and begging for a little water. It turned out that he had jumped and escaped from the truck in which the Jews were being taken from the ghetto to forced labor. When the Germans saw this, they shot him and wounded him severely. With what remained of his strength, he reached the market, with much blood flowing from him. No one helped him, no one tended to him.

Lusia felt a strong desire to run to him and give him a little water to drink. She refrained from approaching him, fearing that she would be caught.

As if history were repeating itself: once again Lusia was left alone with an infant, and once again she had to support herself by her own strength.

She found work sewing linings for fur coats. When she went to work, the caretaker of the residence would feed the infant Dovek and care for him.

Lusia received an "affidavit" (entry and residence permit) to the United States from her husband's family, the Dreier family, and was second in the quota set by the Americans.

From the Land of Israel, her father encouraged her to travel to the house of "Uncle Sam," due to the difficult conditions that prevailed in the Land at that time, and told her that after a year she would be able to join them in the Land.

Lusia refused to distance herself again from her husband and from Shimon and rejected the generous offer.

results.haiku_translation:
At Hitler's Grave
When Lusia was in West Germany, for the purpose of settling her personal compensation, she decided one day that she must close the circle of the terrible years of her past, to see with her own eyes Hitler's grave and to feel satisfaction that the enemy, that terrible monster, was indeed buried and entombed in the earth." She said—and did it. From West Berlin she traveled alone to East Berlin, where Hitler, the enemy of the Jews, was buried.
East Berlin, communist, had not yet recovered since the end of the fighting. Traces of war—ruined buildings, neglected paths, destroyed road infrastructure and deserted streets as after a battle—were evident there at every step.
She arrived at the grave, on which only one word fluttered: "Hitler."
Suddenly, she lost control of herself, felt her body contract, her fists clenched, she gritted her teeth, stood and spat on the grave, spat and screamed: "Do you see, you human monster, murderer like you, I, my husband and my children have remained alive while you lie here, in a grave..."
She spat and spat until she was exhausted and her screaming continued and echoed like rolling thunder. Then she was shocked by the way she had expressed her feelings. She remained alone. A big and brave woman before a pile of stones that covered a murderer. Not a human being but a monster that knew no satiety.
She closed her eyes and said in her heart: "I will never be able to erase this Holocaust from my heart, from my eyes, from my thoughts. One day I will tell my grandchildren, if I have the strength to do so..."
Human language is too poor to express the horrors of the Holocaust, the cry of a Jewish child, the weeping of a beloved mother, the groaning of an infant shattered to death.
Who could describe the loss of a people?!
results.sonnet_translation:
F
By Hitler's Grave

When Lusia stayed in West Germany for the purpose of arranging her personal reparations, she decided one day that she must close the circle of her terrible past years, to see with her own eyes Hitler's grave and to feel satisfaction that the enemy, that terrible monster, was indeed buried and interred in the ground.

She said it — and did it. From West Berlin she traveled alone to East Berlin, where Hitler, the enemy of the Jews, was buried.

East Berlin, the Communist one, had not yet recovered from the end of the fighting. Traces of the war — destroyed houses, neglected paths, ruined road infrastructure and soot-covered streets as after battle — were visible there at every step.

She arrived at the grave, upon which rose only one word: "Hitler."

Suddenly, she lost control of herself, felt her body contract, her fists clenched, she pinched her hands, stood and spat on the grave, spat and screamed: "You see, human filth, murderer like you, I, my husband and my son survived while you lie here, in the grave..."

She spat and spat until her strength was exhausted and her scream continued and continued like a rolling echo. Afterward she was amazed at herself by the way she had expressed her feelings. She remained alone. A great and courageous woman facing a pile of stones that covered a murderer. Not a human being but a monster that knew no satiation.

She closed her eyes and said in her heart: "I will never be able to erase this Holocaust from my heart, from my eyes, from my thoughts. One day I will tell my grandchildren, if I have the strength..."

Human language is too poor to express the horrors of the Holocaust, the cry of a Jewish child, the weeping of a loving mother, the moan of an infant smashed to death.

Who can describe the loss of a people?!
results.translator_notes:
• Historical note: Hitler's actual burial site has never been confirmed. His body was reportedly burned by the Soviets and his remains scattered. There was never a marked grave with his name in East Berlin. This passage appears to reflect Lusia's emotional journey to what she believed or imagined to be his grave, or possibly a symbolic memorial site, rather than an actual historical grave.
• 'חלאת אדם' (khalat adam) - literally 'human filth' or 'scum of humanity'
• The text uses powerful, visceral language to describe Lusia's emotional catharsis at this site
• 'נכדיי' (nikhdai) - 'my grandchildren' - suggesting this memoir itself is partly fulfilling that promise to tell future generations
• The chapter shifts from personal narrative to broader Holocaust remembrance in the final paragraphs
• No OCR issues detected
• Timeline: This visit to Germany would have been post-war, likely in connection with German reparations (Wiedergutmachung) processes
